text.title: mcp-security Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for mcp-security. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: MCP tools that accept SQL inputs MUST validate that only SELECT queries are allowed (regex match on statement start). MCP tools that accept file paths MUST reject paths containing ".." (path traversal). MCP tools that accept URI inputs MUST validate the URI scheme against an allowlist.
rule.rationale: SQL injection, path traversal, and SSRF are the top three MCP input attack vectors. Validation must happen inside the tool handler, not just at the JSON schema layer — JSON Schema validates type and format, not semantic safety.

rule.statement: MCP servers MUST implement tool-level access control: check calling client identity before executing sensitive tools. Read-only tools and write tools MUST NOT share the same permission level. Sensitive operations (file writes, DB mutations, API POSTs) MUST require explicit user consent before execution.
rule.rationale: The MCP protocol itself does not enforce access control — that is the server's responsibility. Without it, any client that connects to the server (including a compromised one) can invoke all tools.

rule.statement: HTTP-transport MCP servers MUST implement bearer token authentication on every endpoint. stdio-transport servers MUST NOT be exposed to the network. Rate limiting MUST be applied to tool invocations in both transports to prevent runaway agent loops.
rule.rationale: stdio is single-client and local; the OS provides the security boundary. HTTP is multi-client and network-accessible; the server is entirely responsible for auth.

rule.statement: Any MCP tool that writes state (file writes, DB mutations, API POSTs) MUST have a human-in-the-loop checkpoint before execution. The tool description MUST declare side-effect scope (e.g., "writes to the database"). Audit logs MUST record caller identity, inputs, and outputs for every tool invocation.
rule.rationale: The model cannot undo tool actions. Once a file is deleted or a record is mutated, rollback requires external intervention. Audit logs are the only post-hoc accountability mechanism.
